deductions.reason: Across all three transcripts, the tutor never explicitly explains why it is guiding rather than giving answers—i.e., it never articulates that the Socratic approach supports active reasoning, retention, or skill development. In Transcript 1 Turn 4 the tutor says 'That is obvious—and that's fine' but does not explain the pedagogical rationale for pushing the student to go deeper. In Transcript 3 Turn 4 the tutor says 'I'm not going to give you a complete sample to copy. My role is to help you build it yourself,' which is the closest to a rationale but is more a statement of role than an explanation of why this supports learning.

In all three transcripts, when students make progress (e.g., Transcript 2 Turn 3 correctly distinguishing GDP vs income per capita; Transcript 3 Turn 10 producing solid placeholder rows), the tutor moves forward without prompting the student to reflect on what changed in their thinking or why the new approach worked. No turn across any transcript asks the student to articulate what helped them get unstuck.

In Transcript 1, the student repeatedly requests a full re-explanation from scratch (Turns 2, 3, 6, 9, 10) and the tutor cycles through similar reframings without meaningfully consolidating or advancing. By Turn 9 and 10 the student is still asking for the whole assignment re-explained, and the tutor's responses (while reasonable) do not break the loop with a fundamentally different strategy such as a concrete task or a direct summary that closes the spiral. The pattern repeats across multiple turns without synthesis.

grade.overview: The tutor performs well on the core Socratic method criteria: it never gives submission-ready answers, never rewrites student text, and consistently asks guiding questions before revealing conclusions. Scaffolding is generally solid—the tutor builds on student progress and diagnoses confusion before responding. The main weaknesses are: (1) no explicit meta-learning rationale is ever offered for why the tutor guides rather than tells; (2) no reflection prompts appear after student breakthroughs; and (3) in Transcript 1 especially, the tutor cycles through similar reframings across many turns without breaking the loop with a fundamentally different strategy, triggering the redundancy/spiraling deduction. Communication quality and assignment anchoring are strong throughout all three transcripts.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never produces near-submission-ready output, never rewrites student text, and consistently withholds conclusions until the student reasons toward them. 1.2 receives full marks: the tutor builds on correct student steps (e.g., affirming the location/area distinction in Transcript 2 Turn 3) and asks diagnostic questions before explaining. 1.3 loses 4 points: no turn across any transcript explains why the Socratic approach benefits the student (1.3.A.a), and no turn prompts reflection after improvement (1.3.C.a). 2.1 loses all 4 points: Transcript 1 shows a persistent loop where the student requests full re-explanation in Turns 2, 3, 6, 9, and 10, and the tutor's reframings, while reasonable, do not break the cycle with synthesis or a fundamentally different move. 2.2, 3.1, and 3.2 are all clean with no deductions.
